Dear Lynda:
This will conf irrn our telephone conversations on January 31
and February 1, 1991. At that time, I contacted you to seek
guidance from your offi.ce on beha]f of my client as to whether
certain actions rnight be considered as lobbying activities 
under
the Act. I indicated that I represented a cal-ifornia nonprofit
corporation whose prirnary program is to influence federal
legislation. 
I asked if certain activities in Cafifornia might
involve lobbying activities and/or contacts, as foflows:
(1) whether one to five enployees of the corporation who
meet with various legislators and/or their staff for the purpose
of introducing the organization to the legislative offices,
providing information regarding the organizationrs purposes in
connection with federal legislation, and possibly inquiring
regarding the legislatorts general position on the particular
subject matter, u/ou1d constitute lobbying under circurnstances
where there is no pending legislation of interest to the
corporation i
(2) whether rneetings with agency officials 
regarding
possibte future regulations would be considered lobbying; and
(3) whether participatj,ng in a press conference with a
State Senator, who indicates at the press conference that he
intends to introduce an unsolicited resolution in the legislature
relating to federal legislation, is a contact or involves
expenses relating to lobbying.
After checking with others in your office, you subsequently
advised me as fol lows:
(1) The rneetings between employees and legislators and
their staff are not considered contacts under the Act for
lobbying purposes, if the purpose of the meetings is to provide
inforrnation, introduce the entity to the legisJ-ators, explain the
organizationrs purposes and positions as to a particular subject

matter, and/or to inquire regarding the l-egislatorrs position on
a particuLar subject or even on a particular bil-1. you indicated
that, for a lobbying contact to occur, the organizationrs
representative must encourage support of or opposition to a
particular bill or piece of legislation.
(2) It is not considered trying to inffuence admj-nistrative
action, and therefore not lobbying, j.f a corporationrs empl,oyees
meet with agency officials to talk about issues, and there are no
proposed regulations. 
You said that under Government Code
Sections 82002 and 82037, the definition of administrative action
requires drafting and other affirmative action regarding proposed
regulations.
(3) Attendance at a press conference is not considered a
lobbying contact because the Senator asks the corporation to
assist and be at the press conference, and because the
corporation is not attempting to inffuence state legislation, but
rather trying to influence the public and the elected official
regarding federal legislative issues. while this activity 
does
not constitute a contact, you did say that the costs incurred by
the corporation in connecti,on with the press conference \,/oul-d
nevertheless be applicable for purposes of filing a Iobbying
discfosure form based on the expenditure of 95,OOO during a
calendar quarter.
We discussed during our conversation the definitions
applicable to a lobbyist, i.e. the compensation test or the time
test, and the requirement for a lobbying organization spendj-ng
$5,000 in a calendar quarter to fil-e a disclosure form. Based on
information from ny client, I indicated that I did not believe
that $5,000 had been spent or that any employee carne cl-ose to
meeting the compensation or the time test. 
This was especialfy
true based on the guidance you provided, which confirmed that any
meetings with legislative officials in their offices and/or in
connection with the press conference, rr/ou1d not be considered
contacts under the facts described. You concurred that it did
not sound like any of the tests had been met by virtue of the
types of the activities described above.
Thank you very much for your guidance.
Very truly
Pn^ 4
Peter A. Ba

sections.question: (1) whether one to five employees of the corporation who meet with various legislators and/or their staff for the purpose of introducing the organization to the legislative offices, providing information regarding the organization's purposes in connection with federal legislation, and possibly inquiring regarding the legislator's general position on the particular subject matter, would constitute lobbying under circumstances where there is no pending legislation of interest to the corporation; (2) whether meetings with agency officials regarding possible future regulations would be considered lobbying; and (3) whether participating in a press conference with a State Senator, who indicates at the press conference that he intends to introduce an unsolicited resolution in the legislature relating to federal legislation, is a contact or involves expenses relating to lobbying.
sections.conclusion: After checking with others in your office, you subsequently advised me as follows: (1) The meetings between employees and legislators and their staff are not considered contacts under the Act for lobbying purposes, if the purpose of the meetings is to provide information, introduce the entity to the legislators, explain the organization's purposes and positions as to a particular subject matter, and/or to inquire regarding the legislator's position on a particular subject or even on a particular bill. You indicated that, for a lobbying contact to occur, the organization's representative must encourage support of or opposition to a particular bill or piece of legislation. (2) It is not considered trying to influence administrative action, and therefore not lobbying, if a corporation's employees meet with agency officials to talk about issues, and there are no proposed regulations. You said that under Government Code Sections 82002 and 82037, the definition of administrative action requires drafting and other affirmative action regarding proposed regulations. (3) Attendance at a press conference is not considered a lobbying contact because the Senator asks the corporation to assist and be at the press conference, and because the corporation is not attempting to influence state legislation, but rather trying to influence the public and the elected official regarding federal legislative issues. While this activity does not constitute a contact, you did say that the costs incurred by the corporation in connection with the press conference would nevertheless be applicable for purposes of filing a lobbying disclosure form based on the expenditure of $5,000 during a calendar quarter.
sections.question_synthetic: The attorney asks whether certain activities by a nonprofit corporation focused on federal legislation—including employee meetings with legislators to introduce the organization, meetings with agency officials about potential regulations, and participation in a press conference—constitute lobbying contacts or activities under California law.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/letter from an attorney to FPPC staff, not an FPPC formal advice letter response. The document contains the attorney's paraphrased summary of verbal guidance received from FPPC staff (Ms. Lynda Cassady) during telephone conversations, not an official FPPC written advice letter with standard Q/C sections. Document lacks formal FPPC letterhead and signature. Treated as incoming request rather than formal advice letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: (1) whether one to five employees of the corporation who meet with various legislators and/or their staff for the purpose of introducing the organization to the legislative offices, providing information regarding the organization's purposes in connection with federal legislation, and possibly inquiring regarding the legislator's general position on the particular subject matter, would constitute lobbying under circumstances where there is no pending legislation of interest to the corporation; (2) whether meetings with agency officials regarding possible future regulations would be considered lobbying; and (3) whether participating in a press conference with a State Senator, who indicates at the press conference that he intends to introduce an unsolicited resolution in the legislature relating to federal legislation, is a contact or involves expenses relating to lobbying.

CONCLUSION: After checking with others in your office, you subsequently advised me as follows: (1) The meetings between employees and legislators and their staff are not considered contacts under the Act for lobbying purposes, if the purpose of the meetings is to provide information, introduce the entity to the legislators, explain the organization's purposes and positions as to a particular subject matter, and/or to inquire regarding the legislator's position on a particular subject or even on a particular bill. You indicated that, for a lobbying contact to occur, the organization's representative must encourage support of or opposition to a particular bill or piece of legislation. (2) It is not considered trying to influence administrative action, and therefore not lobbying, if a corporation's employees meet with agency officials to talk about issues, and there are no proposed regulations. You said that under Government Code Sections 82002 and 82037, the definition of administrative action requires drafting and other affirmative action regarding proposed regulations. (3) Attendance at a press conference is not considered a lobbying contact because the Senator asks the corporation to assist and be at the press conference, and because the corporation is not attempting to influence state legislation, but rather trying to influence the public and the elected official regarding federal legislative issues. While this activity does not constitute a contact, you did say that the costs incurred by the corporation in connection with the press conference would nevertheless be applicable for purposes of filing a lobbying disclosure form based on the expenditure of $5,000 during a calendar quarter.

embedding.summary: Attorney Peter Bagatelos writes to confirm telephone guidance from FPPC staff regarding whether his client nonprofit's activities constitute lobbying under California law, summarizing the FPPC's advice that informational meetings with legislators, agency meetings without proposed regulations, and press conference attendance do not constitute lobbying contacts.
